sentences.text: Around Budapest, that forced labor period when-- when we were involved in that for about a week or 10 days, or so-- can't remember the exact number of days-- it was organized and very, very disorganized.
Because food was occasionally given, but delivery of food for that many people digging trenches and how to reach them and so forth was not-- not done like a well organized military outfit.
So even so, if you were under some degree of some authority and-- and organized people who demanded the work from us, it was never thought out how they feed us.
Occasionally some food arrived.
People were trying to-- to offer above the going price around anywhere where they could get contact with some Gentile people or-- on the towns, or around the suburbs of the city.
And that's how we survived.

And also, when families from the city of Budapest found out from some Gentile people who were willing to deliver some-- some news about our whereabouts, sometime they arrived with some packages of food.
So there were trickling food in somehow that we-- we-- we didn't starve to death.
But we sure were very malnourished.

And another thing which I like to mention-- during the death march, when my father returned with Wallenberg's papers, from the location where we were separated, a day later or two days later, a person, a Gentile, a neighbor from the same building as the Jewish people lived, and interestingly, during while we were there, I knew that he is a very active Arrow Cross party advocate.
But he had a-- a tailor shop.

But from what I understand from my parents, one of his tailors, on bribing him by my mother, was willing to look for us and found my father and had my papers from Wallenberg for this so-called protected house to be transported and released from-- from my unit-- of this forced labor unit.
But I wasn't there anymore.
Because they were separating me from my father.
And then-- then he was-- as I understand from story from my mother, he was on a motorcycle.
And he was following some of this marching miserable thousands of people who were moving along on the highways.
Unbelievable, pitiful scene.
And he could never locate me.
And it was during the time when I did my own trying of escaping.
So he never found me.
And he returned empty-handed, just being able to deliver my father's paper, but not mine.
And when my father arrived back to that protected house, which was a different location, and-- and arrived without me, my mother said, how could you let your child go without you?
You should have gone with him, wherever, to protect him.
And father said, it wasn't my doing.
How do you think I feel?
I remember often that exchange which took place between the two of them.
It was repeated to me when we all survived.
So, father must have had miserable feelings that he lost sight of me and I was separated.
And it takes-- takes me back that the death march was over.
We were put in wagons.
And I didn't understand how or why, but we arrived back to Hungarian speaking part of the world.
And nobody explained much those days to anybody there, the German authorities, and then again, Hungarian authorities who took control of us.
The little gossip and rumor was that our train was then turned back because the railroad tracks were not functioning.
Maybe it was bombed by the Western powers.
And also that Auschwitz, which was the destination, couldn't take more Hungarians, or more-- more Jews.
Because it was just no more room left.
We didn't know about the death machinery of the Nazis in Auschwitz.
But we knew that wherever they were taking us, it wasn't delivered, and we were coming back to the Austria-Hungarian border and put into temporary camps.
It was always just moanings, people who were sick.
It was just hopes that they will get out of those wagons.
It was never really anything meaningful I can recall.
And everybody just silently was waiting for the next minute, the next hour, and the next something, what will happen.
Hard to remember it, really.
It was good to step off and be alive.
Because I thought I was just suffocating and the smell and death among us who died.
It was-- it was an awful thing.
And then stepping off to fresh air, it was relative, something new.
And let's see now what happens.
Next, we were herded away to a town, but the outside of the town, next to the little small town of Harka.
Harka was a border down of Hungary with Austria.
And it had a Main Street.
And it had a-- a-- a church with a tower in the center of town.
It looked like a peaceful Hungarian village.
And-- and we were then herded in the outskirt of the town into barracks, most of them which were used for animals.
But the animals were not there.
And we were placed into barracks.
And then we understood that what we are there for, again, was forced labor.
And we were starting to dig trenches as well as tank trenches again.
And we were given some very basic minimal food daily.
It was much more organized because there was daily food for a while-- for a while.
And-- and we were sleeping on hay.
It was under cover.
And the first week with hard labor, or the first two weeks with hard labor, went by.

And then new transports arrived, which were forced labor units from the Russian border with an order-- well, our group, we were Tomas Stern and a couple of other names-- a name comes to my mind, his name was [?
[RUSSIAN] ?] from the same building.
That few youngsters, we kind of try to keep together.
Because most of the people were not my age-- older.
And so we were the new arrivals, this forced labor units who came from the Russian front.
And they were arriving there in-- in large numbers.
So our camp grew from some 2,000 people to probably 6,000 people in that location.
And when they arrived, they were-- they were bringing something with them which I had no idea that exists.
That something was lice.
Lice.

And lice is deadly. 'Cause lice on they clothes, which they wear for a year in this military, paramilitary units on the Russian fronts, they were lice-infected and that lice was the hotbed of typhoid fever.
And we were all trying to be-- and they told us, try to be clean, not to get lice, because lice can cause typhoid fever.
And what happened is that in the barrack, myself, which I was in, in a week or two, some people got tremendous fever.
They got ill.
They were talking nonsense in the high fever.
Horrible scenes of-- of-- of-- confused people from delusions from high fever.
One of them was running out naked in front of the barrack because his wedding taxi arrives.
I recall that scene.
And-- and I remember the day when I found that I myself have lice on my clothes.
And under the foldings of your little coat, they were like avenues of lice, walking.
And then you found it in your hair, and in your pubic area.
And-- and-- and we were lice-infected.
And more and more people had typhoid fever and died.
And-- and the work, the forced labor work was going on.
We were then out in wintertime, in snow, in ice, with minimal footwear.
Soaked, frozen, wet shoes.
Frozen ice.
People were dying of typhoid fever, from malnutrition, people were-- out on the work were shot at because they didn't work fast enough.
It was a total terror.
Total terror how we were living from one day to the other.
And then people started to have frozen extremities.
Hands, mostly legs, which were getting no circulation.
And-- and-- and were infected.
There was few Jewish doctors who were among the deportees in that place, from the paramilitary units of-- coming from the Russian front, or some of those who were with our Budapest Jewish houses.
These doctors were trying to deliver medical help.
And I, myself, were-- was getting weaker and weaker.
This friend of mine, whom I found in Harka again, and we were sticking together, this Tomas Stern, whose name I mentioned earlier, he was getting weaker and weaker.
We never seen a mirror, but looking at each other, we could tell that we are less and less of a human being any more.
Day after day after day, just what went on, starvation, death.
We were sleeping next to each other in that barrack.
And he was more and more passive.
And one day, this Jewish doctor, who came to make rounds of-- for people, like in a hospital, making rounds, to see why somebody couldn't go out to work.
Because we were, again, organized into units.
And the unit commander was a jupo, Jude Polizei, a Jewish policeman.
In other camps, as I found out later, they called them different.
They were called kapos, camp polizei.
In this location, the name was Jude Polizei, jupo.
These jupos sometimes were rather rough on the Jewish fellows.
And they were making us move and get out and work, because they had to produce numbers, how many people goes.
And freezing cold outside.
Snow.
Ice.
I had a pick which I had to move the earth, the dirt, with.
I-- each of my raising that pick and-- and-- and shovel did not generate too much to achieve.
And the Arrow Cross people and the German guards around us were terrorizing us that I'll kill you if you don't move on.
And-- and the conversation was often German words shouted at us.
[GERMAN].
And stinking Jew, and so on.
And move on and work.
I kill you.
And they were shooting people and so on.
It was-- it was terror, terror, terror.
Starvation, typhoid fever.
It was inhumane.
It was awful.
Time went on.
And one morning this Jewish doctors who came in-- I couldn't get up.
I was very exhausted.
And this Tomas Stern, my friend, said that he is unable to get up and move.
And so these Jewish doctors asked us, well, what's going on?
And they checked him.
And they said that he should be put on a-- a kind of a makeshift stretcher.
And they took him away.
And they said to me, try to get better.
Try to-- try to, not to lay down.
Because the more you lay down, the weaker you get.
And again, next day, I was trying to join the labor unit.
And I didn't see Tomas Stern for awhile.
And then couple of days later, I was at the end of my rope.
I was extremely weak.
I was ill.
I didn't know what-- you know, exhaustion.
And-- and bone-- skin and bone.
I must have lost weight beyond recognition.
So they took me to the same location where I found Tomas Stern.
And that was a small barrack where there was heat.
They had a stove.
And in that location, there were Jewish doctors who, with minimal, minimal medical equipment-- whatever they had in they physician handbags when they were taken away-- they were performing surgery.
They amputated legs and wrapped them in all kind of rags and dirty papers and whatnot.
Because the infection which took place from that frozen and-- and-- and diseased extremity, the only solution was to try to amputate it.
And how do you amputate?
There was no local anesthesia.

And there-- I'm talking somewhat of a specialist-- retroactive about it there, because I'm an anesthesiologist by medical training, which I did in the United States for 35 years just in practice, and so on, which we'll talk about later.
So these people were operated on just until they fainted from pain.
And I do know of one person whose amputation apparently saved him.
Because when I returned to Budapest, and a couple of months later, I bumped into him on his crutches.
He survived.
And he survived without portion of his leg.
So they did good.
These doctors, even with those very primitive conditions.
And sometime, it was in vain and nothing good came of it.
Anyhow, I was getting a little better in a day or two.
Because in that location, there was given a little more of the soup.
And I was in warmer location.
I do not know exactly the other part of my story which I want to tell.
That I was taken out and-- and sent out from that location, back to my unit, into the barrack.

And again, typhoid fever, and being lice everywhere on our clothes and in our hair, the eggs of the-- of the lice was just hanging and it-- you were cutting with whatever little knife somebody could have had, to try to get rid of portions of that.
You know, it was awful-looking, every one of us.
And-- and as a 16-year-old boy, I felt that my beard is somewhat growing, but long pieces of hair were hanging on me, which-- I never shaved.
And-- and then happened one event, which I will never forget, that somebody stole something.
It may have been food.
And they said that somebody should stand up.
And the Germans who were present there were making the investigation and show an example.
And fright and torture can-- can make people speak.
And they said that if nobody comes forward to tell who it was who stole whatever they were looking for, then execution will come.
And they will decimate this group.
Which means every 10th person will be taken out and-- and shot.
So, they did good on their words.
They started to count as we stood.
And I was somewhere on number eight or number nine, and the next person was taken out of line.
Then, four people were taken away, out of this unit, to be executed.
And about six of us were selected to come to witness the execution and bury them.
And then we were walking away from our barracks to a site near to some wooded area where the execution took place.
One of the men said that I have a last wish.
I never forget him.
What is it?
He says, I have with me my [?
[RUSSIAN], ?] my praying [?
[RUSSIAN], ?] my praying shawl.
And I would like to pray.
When he ended, the four of them were executed.
Then we had to dig graves.
And six or eight of us who were there were divided, two here, two here, two here, and start digging graves.
And this awful people who made the execution, Arrow Cross and always a German commander with them.
When they were thrown into the grave, the one which I was standing next to, this Arrow Cross person, says that this body cannot be buried semi-facedown.
You go down and turn him face up.
So reluctantly, I got down the grave, I turned the body, and I only remember the rest of it when I was coming back from darkness.
What I was told I was fainted.
And as I was sitting with the finished graveyards next to me, this other people said that I fainted.
And the Arrow Cross soldier and the German guy said, well, he died, too.
Just bury him.
But they said, no.
He's alive.
He fainted.
And they begged him to help and let-- to be pulled out.
So they eventually convinced him.
They pulled me out.
I was covered with blood and-- and-- and earth, dirt, sticking to me from his blood and so forth.
And then-- then I came out from my shock.
And that's the story they told me, that I was started to be thrown dirt on me to be buried alive when they finally convinced them to give me a chance to live.
Not to go into too much details of-- of experience what went on there, eventually woman groups arrived and joined us in that location of Harka.
And not to really go into details of-- of-- of humiliation, dirt, and sickness, and-- and-- and-- and death, and whatever.
I don't want to be remembered by anybody who will view this or listen to what I'm telling.
But it was very inhumane.
It was the lowest of the low.
We were constantly reminded of our Jewishness by our keepers.
And we were constantly called stinking Jews and-- and awful, awful, awful situation and awful treatment.
From the distance, we could hear humming and bombing.
And we were hoping that something is happening with the war.
We were never able to get information, what's happening and how far the war, and what's going on.
And probably I am jumping here with my memories and-- and telling what happened there.
But the moment came when suddenly they wanted to evacuate the camp.
Well, there were two chiefs of Jewish police who were playing along with the Germans.
They were saving they own skin, I suppose.
And they were the chief Jude Polizei.
I remember their name.
One was Mr.
Kurty and the other one was Mr.
Weinmann.
And those two were following the rules for the Germans pretty much to every demand.
Retrospect, one looks at it, and one-- one wonders, did they have a choice?
And if they had a choice, somebody else would have done the same instead of them.
But I remember these two names.
They were the chief Jewish police.
And they were trying to carry out all kind of measures to provide the number of people they wanted for the tank-- tank entrapments and the-- and all this hard labor.
And sometime they revoked our minimal food allotment.
Just to force us to eat and they-- we will get it when we get to the place to continue digging and-- and-- and working.
And-- and it seemed like that the-- work itself was nothing important anymore, because we achieved practically nothing.
It was frozen dirt and we could not in the snow and the ice achieve much.
It was really a-- an exercise in futility.
And came the time when I started to tell-- the-- from the distance, we started to hear all this rumblings.
You want to ask me something.
Please.
Get up, early in the morning.
Stand outside.
Be accounted for.
Then these Jude Polizei came into the barracks to see if somebody is there, either lying down and-- and not being able to move and sick, or hiding.
Because you went out-- you could have frozen legs-- if you didn't go out, you didn't have food.
It was a hard choice for that day, how to survive.
And it-- just, the routine was survival.
From one day to the other.
We had then seen ladies, women, who arrived.
And there were now women at the camp as well.
And I remember one person who was a very distant relative of mine, whom I recognized there.
But we were never supposed to be close to each other.
So we waved to each other and-- some second cousin from the city of Budapest.
And I do not know any other people there except my immediate barrack people, whose name many of them I remember.
And I should mention there was a man called Lobo.
He was, or at least he claimed to be, an electrician.
And he had some work done for the German headquarters, electrical work, and so on.
And sometime he arrived with some extra food which he was able to gather there.
And then when he came back, he just kind of thrown around here and there to people lying down in the haystack locations and the barrack.
By the way, the haystack was full of lice as well.
It was everywhere disease.
And I remember another one who was always very strong, that everybody should keep moving.
Because lying down is death.
And his name was Stein.
And he was a very deep-voiced man-- who who was surviving that portion of the concentration camp and labor camp part, but he never made the rest of it.
Which I continue telling you about.
